content_en:
Lisan al-Mizan achieved the status of the definitive reference for criticized narrators in the broader hadith literature — those beyond the narrators covered in the six canonical collections (addressed in Tahdhib at-Tahdhib). Together with Mizan al-I'tidal, it provides the most comprehensive coverage of the narrator-criticism tradition available in a usable reference format, and the two works are regularly consulted together by scholars evaluating the reliability of specific hadith.

Ibn Hajar's authority in the hadith sciences — reinforced by his position as the author of Fath al-Bari, the greatest commentary on Sahih al-Bukhari, and of Tahdhib at-Tahdhib and Taqrib at-Tahdhib — gave his assessments in Lisan al-Mizan particular weight. Later scholars who found themselves with a narrator whose reliability was in doubt turned first to Ibn Hajar's works for guidance, and his judgments in Lisan al-Mizan were treated as among the most authoritative available.

In the modern period, Lisan al-Mizan has become even more valuable as digital tools have made it possible to search and cross-reference it with the broader rijal literature. Researchers who want to evaluate the narrator-criticism tradition systematically — to identify patterns of evaluation, to study the methodology of specific critics, or to trace the history of assessments for a specific narrator — find Lisan al-Mizan an essential part of the digital corpus they analyze.

The work's coverage of narrators in non-canonical sources — Sufi, Shia, and regional biographical works — has attracted particular attention from scholars studying the margins of the hadith tradition and the ways in which the mainstream Sunni authentication methodology engaged with or excluded different communities of transmission. For students of Islamic studies, Lisan al-Mizan offers a rich case study in how canonical boundaries were negotiated in practice, as Ibn Hajar's willingness to evaluate narrators from across the sectarian and doctrinal spectrum shows both the reach of the rijal sciences and the confidence of the tradition in applying its methodology wherever hadith-related transmission could be identified.
